content.full_text:
Farn Polrrrcer- PRAcrrces C oMMrssroN
428 J Street r Suite 620 o Sacramenro, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
January 8, 2008
John G. Barisone
City Attorney, City of Capitola
333 Church Street
Santa Cruz, California 95060
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-07-198
Dear Mr. Barisone:
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Capitola City
Councilmember Michael Termini regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act")l and is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political
Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders
advice. (ln re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, the Commission will not
advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A), copy enclosed.)
Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may
have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to
prospective actions.
Finally, we base our advice solely on the provisions of the Act and do not address
the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of
interest or Government Code Section 1090.
QUESTION
May Councilmember Termini return income his business received from a person
that is the subject of a proceeding before his agency so that he may participate in the
decision without a conflict of interest under the Act?
CONCLUSION
I The Political Reform Act is contained in Governmenr Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through lti997 of Title 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2. Division 6 of the Calitbrnia Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
u;:,+l


File No. A-07-198
Page No. 2
No. The Act does not provide for the retum of income in order to avoid a
financial interest that would prohibit the official from participating in a govemmental
decision under the confl ict-of-interest provisions.
FACTS
Capitola City Councilmember Michael Termini is the president and sole
shareholder of Triad Electric, Inc., a closely held California corporation that conducts
electrical contracting work. Triad Electric, Inc. contracts and subcontracts for electrical
installations on construction projects and also contracts to perform electrical repair and
maintenance work on existing structures. Councilmember Termini has served on the
Capitola City Council since December 2004 and, before his service on the city council,
he served on the Capitola Planning Commission between December 2000 and December
2004. The members of the Capitola City Council also sit as Directors for the City of
capitola Redevelopment Agency and, accordingly, since December zoo4,
Councilmember Termini has also served as a member of the Board of Directors for the
City of Capitola Redevelopment Agency.
During Councilmember Termini's tenure on the Capitola Planning Commission, a
private developer - Rispin Partners LLC ("Rispin"), entered into an agreement with the
redevelopment agency whereby Rispin would be authorized to apply for and process an
application for redevelopment of a bed and breakfast/spa project on redevelopment
agency-owned property known as the Rispin property. The proposed project, popularly
referred to as the Rispin Mansion Project ("Project"), entailed the restoration o1a historic
mansion on the property for this use. If approved, the City, the redevelopment agency,
and Rispin would enter into a long-term agreement whereby Rispin would operate the
bed and breakfast/spa establishment on the site. The proposed Project entailid many
complexities ranging from construction technicalities, environmental issues, historic
preservation, financing issues, and legal issues. As a result, the development application
for the project was the subject of multiple public hearings during Councilmember
Termini's tenure on both the planning commission and city council. Ultimately, in 2005
the development application was approved by the city council and, in addition, both the
redevelopment agency and the city council approved a lease/development agreement with
Rispin pursuant to which the approved development would take place and Rispin would
thereafter proceed to operate the bed and breakfast/spa establishment.
ln 2006, Rispin entered into an agreement with Green Valley Corporation ('Green
Valley") pursuant to which Rispin and Green Valley, would proceed in a partnership to
develop the Project and thereafter operate the bed and breakfast/spa estabiishment in
accordance with the terms and conditions specified in the lease development agreement.
The city council and redevelopment agency both consented to the formation of the
partnership and the partnership's assumption of responsibility for development and
operation of the Project.


File No. A-07-198
Page No. 3
Green Valley also does business as Barry Swenson Builder ("Swenson"). Green
Valley Corporation dba Barry Swenson Builder is a single legal entity and operates as a
general construction contractor and is also in the real property development and property
management business. It owns and operates numerous commercial divelopm"nr proi""i,
in the Santa Clara County/SantaCruz County region.
During 2007, unbeknownst to Councilmember Termini, Swenson, pursuant to an
on-call arrangement with one of Councilmember Termini's employees at Triad Electric,
retained the business on five separate occasions to perform electrical repair and
maintenance work on a commercial building owned by Green Valley. iursuant to the
work performed on these service calls, the last of which occurred on Novemb er 29,2007,
Swenson paid Triad Electric $8,706.69.
The city council has routinely received status reports on the Project and
considered project permit amendments to facilitate the project's development. A number
of public hearings of this nature were conducted by the city council and the
redevelopment agency during 2007. Councilmember Termini first leamed that Triad
Electric had performed the electrical service/maintenance work for after the issuance of
the fifth invoice to Swenson on November 29,2007 . It was only at this juncture that
Councilmember Termini learned that Green Valley and Swenson were not separate legal
entities. Upon realizing that Swenson was a source of income to him, Councilmember
Termini immediately proceeded to disqualify himself from further participation in any
city council or redevelopment agency discussions or decisions concerning the project.
Councilmember Termini would like to know if, at this juncture, he returns the
$8,706.69 his company received from Swenson, would the reimbursement function to
eliminate his disqualifying conflict of interest by nullifying his economic interest in
Swenson as a source of income to him and allow him to resume participation in future
city council and redevelopment agency discussions and decisioni pertaining to the
Project.
AI\ALYSIS
PorBNrru, CoNrlrcr oF INTEREsT
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused bytheir own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a gorernmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest, unless an exception applies.
The Commission has adopted an eighrstep standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regularion f SZOO(U).) The general
rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official mak-es a


File No. A-07-198
Page No.4
governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one
or more of his or her financial interests. (Section 97103.)
Under the facts you have presented, Councilmember Termini is a public official
(Step 1) who will be called upon to make, participate in the making ol or influence a
governmental decision (Step 2) in which he has a disqualifying source of income
economic interest. (Step 3). His economic interest, SwensonJs directly involved (Step
4) in the governmental decision as a partner in the Project. As a result, ihe decision is
presumed to have a material financial effect (Step 5) on Councilmember Termini's
economic interest and that effect is reasonably foreseeable (Step 6). Finally, it does not
appear that either the."public generally exception" (step 7) or tire ;,legally iequired
participation" exception (Step 8) would allow Councilmember Termini to participate
despite his conflict of interest.
Section 82030(a) defines income as:
"(a) '[ncom"- T:ur:, except as provided in subdivision (b), a payment
received, including but not limited to any salary, wage, advance, dividend,
interest, rent, proceeds from any sale, gift, including any gift of food or
beverage, loan, forgiv-eness or payment of indebtednessreceived by the
filer, reimbursement for expenses, per diem, or contribution to an
insurance or pension program paid by any person other than an employer,
and including any community property interest in the income of a spouse.
Income also includes an outstanding loan.,'
Sectiort 82030(b) provides exceptions to what is considered income. Unlike the
exceptions for gifts (see Section 82028(b)), there is no exception for income returned.
Accordingly, because the payment was "received" and there is no provision under the Act
to allow the payment to be returned by Councilmember Termini and thereby not be
considered income, under the Act the payment is income whether it is returned or not.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Counsel
WJL:jgl
By:
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

decision under the confl ict-of-interest provisions.
